title: Rigid heddle Tencel scarf
subtitle: A 180 × 30 cm plain-weave scarf in 8/2 Tencel warp and weft, with a lustrous, draping hand
excerpt: Tencel (lyocell) is a manufactured fibre made from wood pulp. It has a silky surface, a pronounced sheen, and a drapey hand that is quite different from wool. At 8/2 weight on a rigid heddle at 15 EPI, it produces a plain-weave scarf that softens further with each wash.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from rigid heddle weaving references and manufacturer care guidance for Tencel (lyocell) fibre.

glossaryTerms.definition: The set of threads stretched lengthwise on the loom under tension, through which the weft is interlaced.
The thread carried horizontally across the warp on each pick (row) of weaving.
The number of warp threads per unit of width, stated in ends per inch (EPI) or ends per centimetre.
On a rigid heddle loom, the slotted and holed plate that holds the warp threads and creates the shed when lifted or lowered.
The V-shaped opening between raised and lowered warp threads through which the shuttle passes.
A single pass of the weft through the shed.
The woven edge of the cloth on each side, formed by the weft looping around the outermost warp threads.
The most basic weave structure: the weft passes over one warp thread and under the next, alternating on each pick.
Washing and pressing the woven cloth off the loom to set the fibres, even out the weave, and achieve the intended hand.
The reduction in warp length caused by the interlacing of weft threads as weaving progresses, typically expressed as a percentage of the total warp.

content.text: Before the draft, here is a plain-English description of how a rigid heddle loom works. The rigid heddle is a plate with alternating holes and slots across its width. Each
warp
thread passes through either a hole or a slot. When the heddle is lifted, the hole threads rise and the slot threads stay low, creating one
shed
. When the heddle is lowered, the slot threads rise and the hole threads stay low, creating the second shed. Alternating up and down on each pass of the shuttle produces
plain weave
Tencel (lyocell) has a silky surface that behaves differently from wool or cotton: it is more slippery in the
heddle
and at the
selvedge
, and it abrades heddle surfaces faster than most fibres. Inspect the heddle after each project and replace if the slots show noticeable wear. The
sett
for 8/2 Tencel on the rigid heddle is 15 EPI (6 ends/cm), achieved with a 15-dent heddle at 1 thread per slot or hole. Allow 10%
take-up
in the width and 12% shrinkage in the length after
wet finishing
. Warp to a length of approximately 230 cm (including loom waste) and a width of 34 cm in the heddle. Total warp ends: 34 cm × 6 ends/cm = approximately 204 ends.

items.name: Rigid heddle loom with 34 cm or wider weaving width

content.text: The draft

definition.title: Rigid heddle plain weave: Tencel scarf

content.text: Reading the draft: shaft 1 represents hole threads (raised when the heddle goes up) and shaft 2 represents slot threads (raised when the heddle goes down). The treadling alternates 1, 2 for each
pick
, producing plain weave throughout.
Warping and threading

Wind 204 ends on the warping board to 230 cm. Tencel is slippery: wind at a firm, even tension and use a figure-of-eight cross to prevent the warp threads from sliding against each other during winding. Thread alternate ends through holes and slots across the 34 cm width of the heddle. Tie the warp onto the front apron rod in small, even bouts. Thread onto the back apron rod the same way and beam under firm tension.

Weave 3 cm of plain weave as a header using a scrap yarn to spread the warp. Remove the scrap yarn after weaving 2 cm of the main weft colour. This header can be folded back and hemstitched, or left as a short fringe.
Weaving the pattern

Lift the heddle to the upper position to open the first shed. Pass the shuttle through. Lower the heddle to the down position to open the second shed. Pass the shuttle back through. This alternation (up, down, up, down) continues for the full length of the scarf.

Beat gently after each pick: Tencel plain weave at 15 EPI should be beaten to approximately 6 picks per cm for a balanced cloth. A heavier beat produces a weft-faced cloth with less drape; a lighter beat produces an open, loosely structured cloth. Lay the
weft
in a shallow arc before beating to avoid pulling the selvedges inward.

Continue weaving until the on-loom length reaches approximately 205 cm (to allow for 12% shrinkage to 180 cm finished). For a striped scarf, change the weft colour as often as every 2 picks or as rarely as every 10 cm: Tencel colour stripes are clear and precise due to the smooth surface of the yarn.
Finishing
Weave 3 cm of plain weave and work a hemstitch at both ends before cutting from the loom. Trim fringe to 3 cm, or knot in pairs close to the hemstitched edge.

Wet finish by hand-washing in cool water (30 °C) with a gentle detergent. Tencel can be washed at up to 40 °C, but 30 °C prevents any unexpected shrinkage on the first wash. Swirl gently, then rinse thoroughly at the same temperature. Roll in a towel to remove excess water. Lay flat or drape over a rail to dry: do not peg by a corner as the wet cloth is heavy and may distort. Press with a warm iron while still slightly damp. The scarf will soften considerably after the first wash and again after each subsequent wash.

items.problem: Warp threads slipping out of alignment during beaming
items.solution: Tencel is very slippery. Make sure the figure-of-eight cross is preserved on lease sticks during beaming, and apply more tension when winding the warp onto the back beam.
The weft arc is too wide. Reduce the arc slightly and re-check the tension. With Tencel's slick surface, the weft glides through the shed easily and does not need as wide an arc as wool.

Tencel abrades heddle surfaces faster than most fibres. This is a known characteristic of smooth cellulosic yarns. Inspect the heddle after every project and replace when slots show noticeable roughness, which can cause warp thread damage.

items.problem: Cloth loses its drape after ironing
items.solution: The iron was too hot or too dry. Use a damp pressing cloth and a medium-heat iron. Tencel can be lightly starched while pressing to add body if a crisper drape is wanted.
